papers.title: FDA recalls several Ghirardelli powdered beverages over potential contamination
papers.abstract: Several powdered beverage mixes by Ghirardelli Chocolate Company are being recalled due to possible concerns of Salmonella contamination.

papers.title: Reverse engineering ketamine's effects may lead to new antidepressants
papers.abstract: Weill Cornell Medicine investigators have "reverse engineered" ketamine's antidepressant effects to identify potential new strategies for treating depression. While there are many effective treatments available for depression, not all patients respond to them.

papers.title: Battery-free skin-conformal wearable system can measure electrocardiogram signals
papers.abstract: A research team led by Prof. Jerald Yoo from the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at Seoul National University (SNU) has developed a skin-conformal wearable health care system, "SkinECG," capable of measuring electrocardiogram (ECG) signals without a battery. By combining energy harvesting with human body–coupled power transfer, the study presents a new solution to one of the most critical challenges in wearable devices: power supply.

papers.title: Early brain regions play greater role in decision-making, challenging traditional neuroscience
papers.abstract: New insight into decision-making pathways in the brain may impact the way engineers think about artificial intelligence, according to new research from The Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Led by electrical and computer engineering professor Yurii Vlasov and published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Science, the group's findings highlight the involvement of early brain regions in decision-making, challenging long-held assumptions about brain hierarchy.

papers.title: Skull microchannels reveal hidden route for brain immune defense
papers.abstract: A study led by Rafael Gallareto-Sande, a predoctoral researcher at the Centro Nacional de Investigación sobre la Evolución Humana (CENIEH), provides new insights into a network of tiny blood vessels within the cranial bones that play a key role in brain protection. The research, published in The Anatomical Record, reports for the first time the number and size of these microscopic channels, known as vascular microforamina, which connect the cranial bone with the intracranial space.

papers.title: Faster and easier ways to diagnose Mpox: New approaches improve detection
papers.abstract: Following the rise in Mpox cases, particularly in countries where the disease had not traditionally been observed, the World Health Organization (WHO) declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern in June 2022 and again in August 2024. Accurate and timely diagnosis plays a critical role in controlling the infection. However, PCR-based methods—the gold standard for Mpox diagnosis—require complex laboratory infrastructure and trained personnel, making them less accessible in many settings. For this reason, the development of point-of-care diagnostic tools is of great importance.

papers.title: First psychiatric admission marks the beginning of a long-term illness for most patients
papers.abstract: Only a very small number of people never return to psychiatric services after being admitted once. That is the conclusion of a new study from the University of Copenhagen, which followed 150 young people for 20 years after their first psychiatric admission.

papers.title: High-intensity exercise after breast cancer surgery may help speed recovery
papers.abstract: After breast cancer surgery, many women are told to limit how much—and how soon—they exercise.

papers.title: Why some aplastic anemia patients recover: Protective blood stem cell clones may restore marrow
papers.abstract: Aplastic anemia is a rare, life-threatening blood disorder where patients are unable to make enough blood cells due to the immune system's attack on blood stem cells. The condition can progress to myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and leukemia.

papers.title: A virtual copy of your brain? Scientists say it's closer than you think
papers.abstract: For years, the idea of building a working replica of the human brain has lived comfortably in the realm of science fiction. A digital double that could think, learn, or even predict the future of a person's health seemed more cinematic than scientific.

papers.title: Ticked off: How a bite can turn meat against you
papers.abstract: Mammalian meat allergy (MMA) is one of the few known food allergies caused by an environmental trigger—a tick bite. In simple terms, MMA results in an allergic reaction to red meat, making it difficult or sometimes even impossible for affected individuals to enjoy meat without experiencing adverse symptoms.

papers.title: Mothers without a specific fatty acid in the blood more often have children with asthma, study reveals
papers.abstract: Shortness of breath, coughing, and respiratory infections. Childhood asthma, also known as asthmatic bronchitis, is one of the most common chronic diseases in children worldwide. But why does the disease develop? Research shows that conditions in the very early phase of life—already during pregnancy—are likely to be decisive.

papers.title: Experts offer perspective on link between pesticide exposure and early-onset colorectal cancer
papers.abstract: Advanced technologies are helping researchers identify environmental contaminants that could potentially be contributing to the rising incidence of early-onset colorectal cancer. However, epigenetic studies on such associations should be interpreted with caution, according to researchers at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, who are leading experts in the disease.

papers.title: CDK inhibitors show wider cancer potential as combinations and biomarkers advance
papers.abstract: Worldwide, more than a million breast cancer patients have been treated with cyclin-dependent kinase 4/6 (CDK4/6) inhibitors, which slow or stop the uncontrolled multiplication of cancer cells. Researchers see untapped potential in those targeted therapies, which can dramatically extend progression-free survival—the time before the disease grows worse—and overall survival, even in metastatic disease. Could those benefits be extended to patients with other types of cancer?

papers.title: Did you know you can’t steal a charity? Don’t worry. Elon Musk will remind you.
papers.abstract: Elon Musk spent the better part of three days on the witness stand this week in his lawsuit against OpenAI, and it’s already getting messy. Emails, texts, and his own tweets are surfacing in court, and there are plenty more witnesses to come. Musk’s argument against OpenAI? By converting the company to a for-profit model, Sam Altman betrayed the “nonprofit for the […]

papers.title: Pentagon inks deals with Nvidia, Microsoft, and AWS to deploy AI on classified networks
papers.abstract: The deals come as the DOD has doubled down on diversifying its exposure to AI vendors in the wake of its controversial dispute with Anthropic over usage terms of its AI models.

papers.title: Musk v. Altman is just getting started
papers.abstract: Elon Musk spent the better part of three days on the witness stand this week in his lawsuit against OpenAI, and it’s already getting messy. Emails, texts, and his own tweets are surfacing in court, and there are plenty more witnesses to come. Musk’s argument against OpenAI? By converting the company to a for-profit model, Sam Altman betrayed the “nonprofit for the […]

papers.title: ChatGPT Images 2.0 is a hit in India, but not a big winner elsewhere, yet
papers.abstract: Users in India are embracing ChatGPT Images 2.0 for creative, personal visuals — from avatars to cinematic portraits.

papers.title: Beacon Biosignals is mapping the brain during sleep
papers.abstract: Founded by Jake Donoghue PhD ’19 and former MIT researcher Jarrett Revels, the company is creating an AI-driven platform to help diagnose and treat disease.

papers.title: Improving understanding with language
papers.abstract: MIT senior Olivia Honeycutt investigates how the ways we communicate can shape our views of the world.

papers.title: Sources: Anthropic potential $900B+ valuation round could happen within 2 weeks
papers.abstract: Anthropic is asking investors to submit allocations for the AI company’s latest fundraise within the next 48 hours, according to sources familiar with the matter.

papers.title: Apple was surprised by AI-driven demand for Macs
papers.abstract: Apple said it will be supply-constrained on Mac mini, Studio, and Neo in the next quarter, too.

papers.title: Legal AI startup Legora hits $5.6B valuation and its battle with Harvey just got hotter
papers.abstract: The two wildly fast-growing rivals have raised massive sums, pushed into each other's home turf, and now have dueling ad campaigns.

papers.title: After dissing Anthropic for limiting Mythos, OpenAI restricts access to Cyber, too
papers.abstract: OpenAI will begin rolling out its cybersecurity testing tool, GPT-5.5 Cyber only "to critical cyber defenders" at first.

papers.title: OpenAI announces new advanced security for ChatGPT accounts, including a partnership with Yubico
papers.abstract: OpenAI is launching additional opt-in protections for ChatGPT accounts. The new security initiative includes a new partnership with security key provider Yubico.

papers.title: Elon Musk testifies that xAI trained Grok on OpenAI models
papers.abstract: "Distillation" is a hot topic as frontier labs try to prevent smaller competitors from copying their models.

papers.title: FDA approval, fundraising, and the reality of building in healthcare according to BioticsAI founder
papers.abstract: BioticsAI CEO Robhy Bustami joined Isabelle Johannessen on Build Mode to discuss how the company has navigated a highly regulated space and kept the team motivated while cutting through all the red tape.

papers.title: Google’s Gemini AI assistant is hitting the road in millions of vehicles
papers.abstract: The move signals Google’s push to bring more advanced, conversational AI into the driving experience.

papers.title: Stripe updates Link, a digital wallet that autonomous AI agents can use, too
papers.abstract: Link lets users connect cards, banks, and subscriptions, then authorize AI agents to spend securely via approval flows.

papers.title: Salesforce is crowdsourcing its AI roadmap — with customers
papers.abstract: Salesforce lets its customers lead its product roadmap with the thinking that if one enterprise customer has a problem, the others likely do too.

papers.title: Making the case for curiosity-driven science
papers.abstract: President Sally Kornbluth spoke in front of a packed crowd about growing challenges to the U.S. research ecosystem as funding for America’s top research universities becomes increasingly strained.

papers.title: Solving the “Whac-a-mole dilemma”: A smarter way to debias AI vision models
papers.abstract: A new debiasing technique called WRING avoids creating or amplifying biases that can occur with existing debiasing approaches.

papers.title: The MIT-IBM Computing Research Lab launches to shape the future of AI and quantum computing
papers.abstract: Building on a long-standing MIT–IBM collaboration, the new lab will chart the convergence of AI, algorithms, and quantum computing.

papers.title: Enabling privacy-preserving AI training on everyday devices
papers.abstract: A new method could bring more accurate and efficient AI models to high-stakes applications like health care and finance, even in under-resourced settings.

papers.abstract: Dolphin or porpoise? How to tell the difference Dolphins and porpoises are both cetaceans, but they have distinct characteristics that set them apart. Learn how to recognize a dolphin vs. a porpoise. (Image credit: NOAA Fisheries) Download Image April 29, 2026 Fisheries marine mammals 0 Off

papers.abstract: Artemis II returns: How NOAA helped protect astronauts during the 10-day NASA mission NASA’s Artemis II astronauts returned to Earth April 10 after a 10-day journey beyond the magnetosphere during which NOAA scientists monitored solar activity for radiation risks. (Image credit: NASA/nasa.gov) April 28, 2026 Satellites space satellites 0 Off

papers.title: One year of GOES-19 as the new GOES-East satellite
papers.abstract: One year of GOES-19 as the new GOES-East satellite It’s been one year since NOAA’s latest geostationary satellite, GOES-19, entered service as GOES-East. Since then, the satellite has provided critical data for high-impact events across the country. (Image credit: NOAA Satellites) April 28, 2026 Satellites geostationary satellite (GOES) satellite imagery and visualizations 0 Off

papers.title: A faster way to estimate AI power consumption
papers.abstract: The “EnergAIzer” method generates reliable results in seconds, enabling data center operators to efficiently allocate resources and reduce wasted energy.

papers.title: Event-based Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: A Comprehensive Survey
papers.authors: Kunping Huang, Sen Zhang, Jing Zhang, Dacheng Tao
papers.abstract: Abstract In recent decades, visual simultaneous localization and mapping (vSLAM) has gained significant interest in both academia and industry. It estimates camera motion and reconstructs the environment concurrently using visual sensors on a moving robot. However, conventional cameras are limited by hardware, including motion blur and low dynamic range, which can negatively impact performance in challenging scenarios like high-speed motion and high dynamic range illumination. Recent studies have demonstrated that event cameras, a new type of bio-inspired visual sensor, offer advantages such as high temporal resolution, dynamic range, low power consumption, and low latency. This paper presents a timely and comprehensive review of event-based vSLAM algorithms that exploit the benefits of asynchronous and irregular event streams for localization and mapping tasks. The review covers the...

papers.title: International Journal of Complementary & Alternative Medicine
papers.abstract: There is a growing demand for integrative and complementary therapies for various health conditions, while the mechanisms and biological pathways for some of these therapies are poorly explored.In particular, bioelectric, biomagnetic and anthropogenic fields interact with biological structures such as tissues, cells and DNA.It is suggested that these interactions may create an integrated system of internal communication, with the potential to promote therapeutic benefits.The theoretical model Biofield-Tissue Tensegrity Matrix (BTTM) is presented by combining concepts from physics, biology and medicine to serve as a possible theoretical basis for energy therapies.
papers.journal: International Journal of Complementary & Alternative Medicine
papers.source: International Journal of Complementary & Alternative Medicine

papers.title: Bias in Large Language Models: Origin, Evaluation, and Mitigation
papers.authors: Yufei Guo, Muzhe Guo, Juntao Su, Zhou Yang, Mengqiu Zhu, Hongfei Li
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing, but their susceptibility to biases poses significant challenges. This comprehensive review examines the landscape of bias in LLMs, from its origins to current mitigation strategies. We categorize biases as intrinsic and extrinsic, analyzing their manifestations in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks. The review critically assesses a range of bias evaluation methods, including data-level, model-level, and output-level approaches, providing researchers with a robust toolkit for bias detection. We further explore mitigation strategies, categorizing them into pre-model, intra-model, and post-model techniques, highlighting their effectiveness and limitations. Ethical and legal implications of biased LLMs are discussed, emphasizing potential harms in real-world applications such as healthcare and crimi...

papers.title: MIT scientists build the world’s largest collection of Olympiad-level math problems, and open it to everyone
papers.abstract: New dataset of 30,000-plus competition math problems from 47 countries gives AI researchers a harder test — and students worldwide a better training ground.

papers.title: Teaching AI models to say “I’m not sure”
papers.abstract: A new training method improves the reliability of AI confidence estimates without sacrificing performance, addressing a root cause of hallucination in reasoning models.

papers.title: Citizen scientists wanted: Find a project that's right for you
papers.abstract: Citizen scientists wanted: Find a project that's right for you NOAA offers a variety of volunteer opportunities that can be done outdoors and online. Meet some of the decided volunteers who make NOAA's citizen science efforts possible. (Image credit: Courtesy of Eva L., Linda Palewicz, and Mary-Sue Atkinson) Download Image April 21, 2026 April is Citizen Science Month, and NOAA has launched a new, searchable catalog of citizen science projects to connect volunteers with research opportunities across the country. The catalog makes it easier for individuals, educators, and community groups to discover and participate in projects that are fun, educational, and contribute to NOAA’s scientific mission, including weather observation, marine ecosystem monitoring, sea floor mapping, and more. There are many ways to get involved, whether you’re getting outdoors or participating on your compute...

papers.title: Scientists reveal identity of mysterious ‘golden orb’ collected during NOAA expedition
papers.abstract: Scientists reveal identity of mysterious ‘golden orb’ collected during NOAA expedition A close up image of the “golden orb” in the lab of the Smithsonian Institution.
